g.data-narration: Welcome to Solar Energy - one of the most abundant renewable energy sources shaping our sustainable future.
The sun provides 1.7 × 10^14 kilowatts continuously, far more than global energy consumption combined.
g.text: • Exceeds all global energy needs combined

g.data-narration: Solar energy is used in two main ways: thermal systems for heat and photovoltaic systems for electricity generation.
g.text: Converts sunlight → Heat energy (water heating, space heating)
Converts sunlight → Electricity (PV cells, panels, systems)

g.data-narration: Solar radiation travels at 3 × 10⁸ m/s with a solar constant of 1367 W/m² at Earth's atmosphere boundary.
The solar spectrum has UV at 7%, visible light at 47%, and infrared at 46%. Air mass affects radiation path.
Solar thermal systems capture radiation and convert to heat using collectors with selective absorbing surfaces.
A flat-plate collector has absorber plate, transparent cover, insulation, and housing for capturing both direct and diffuse radiation.
g.text: where Qu = useful heat, G = radiation, A = area

g.data-narration: Concentrating collectors use mirrors to focus sunlight onto a receiver, achieving high temperatures for power generation.
The photovoltaic effect generates electricity when photons with energy greater than the bandgap strike a semiconductor junction.
g.text: h = Planck's constant, c = speed of light, λ = wavelength

g.data-narration: The I-V curve shows PV cell performance with key parameters: short-circuit current, open-circuit voltage, and fill factor for quality.
Grid-connected PV systems feed excess power to the grid, while off-grid systems use batteries for storage during nighttime.
Energy storage addresses solar intermittency through thermal storage like molten salts, and battery storage systems for round-the-clock power.
g.text: Molten salts, concrete, PCM • For thermal power plants
Lead-acid, Li-ion, Flow batteries • For off-grid PV systems

g.data-narration: Solar passive design uses building orientation, thermal mass, and shading for natural heating and cooling without mechanical systems.
Let's apply the formulas to size a PV system: calculate module count and battery capacity for a 2 kWh per day load requirement.
Review key formulas: collector efficiency, PV efficiency, battery capacity, and PV array sizing for your engineering exams.
g.text: PV size = Daily Energy / (Peak hrs × efficiency)

g.data-narration: Solar energy is abundant, sustainable, and crucial for our future. Master these concepts for your engineering exams and real-world applications.
